items.q: Did the English navy sink most of the Spanish Armada in battle?
items.correct: No - relatively few Spanish ships were sunk in the fighting; most losses came from storms that wrecked ships sailing home around Scotland and Ireland.
items.misconception: Drake destroyed the Armada in one decisive sea battle.
items.random_wrong: The Armada was defeated because Spain had no warships left after 1580.
items.why: The patriotic legend credits English gunnery alone; weather and Spanish supply problems were at least as important, which exam answers must weigh.

items.q: Were all Elizabethan Catholics plotting against the queen?
items.correct: No - most English Catholics stayed loyal; harsh laws targeted missionary priests and recusants, but actual plots involved only a small minority.
items.misconception: Every Catholic in Elizabethan England supported plots to kill Elizabeth.
items.random_wrong: Catholicism was unknown in England by the 1580s.
items.why: Government propaganda blurred the line between faith and treason, and students repeat it; distinguishing loyal Catholics from plotters shows real understanding.

items.q: Did the Elizabethan Poor Laws punish everyone who was poor?
items.correct: No - they distinguished the 'deserving' poor, helped by parish relief, from able-bodied 'idle' vagrants, who could be punished or set to work.
items.misconception: Elizabethans whipped or imprisoned all poor people equally.
items.random_wrong: The Poor Laws abolished poverty in England by 1603.
items.why: The deserving/undeserving distinction is the key idea of Tudor poverty policy and the one students most often miss.

items.q: Why was Mary, Queen of Scots executed in 1587?
items.correct: Evidence implicated her in the Babington Plot to assassinate Elizabeth
items.misconception: She had claimed the throne of France
items.random_wrong: She refused to convert to Catholicism
items.why: It's Evidence implicated her in the Babington Plot to assassinate Elizabeth. “She had claimed the throne of France” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What helped defeat the Spanish Armada in 1588?
items.correct: Fireships scattered the fleet at Calais and storms wrecked many ships on the voyage home
items.misconception: The Spanish fleet ran out of food before leaving Spain
items.random_wrong: A French fleet destroyed the Armada in the Channel
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Fireships scattered the fleet at Calais and storms wrecked many ships on the voyage home. “The Spanish fleet ran out of food before leaving Spain” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What did the 1601 Poor Law require?
items.correct: Parishes to collect a poor rate to support their own deserving poor
items.misconception: All poor people to enter royal workhouses
items.random_wrong: Monasteries to feed the poor of every town
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Parishes to collect a poor rate to support their own deserving poor. “All poor people to enter royal workhouses” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which playhouse, associated with Shakespeare's company, opened in London in 1599?
items.why: Correct answer: The Globe. “The Rose” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did the Spanish Armada sail against England?
items.why: Exact answer: 1588 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
